coredata.dc:title: Hepatotoxicity evaluations of different surface charged carbon quantum dots in vivo and in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yi-Chun
Chen, Hung-Hsiang
Lin, Han-Jia
Huang, Chih-Ching
Chen, Ku-Fan
Peng, Yen-Ping
Tsang, Yiu Fai
Chen, Yan-Hua
Lin, Kun-Yi Andrew
Lin, Chia-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become popular because of their simple synthesis and potential applications. Although CQDs have high biocompatibility, their biotoxicity must be verified to reduce the possible risks associated with large-scale application. In this study, the hepatotoxicity of three CQD types, namely diammonium citrate (AC)-based (CQDs-AC), spermidine trihydrochloride (Spd)-based (CQDs-Spd), and AC- and Spd-based CQDs (CQDs-AC/Spd), were evaluated in vivo and in vitro. It was observed in vivo that CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd, but not CQDs-AC, caused histopathological damage, including liver steatosis and mild mixed inflammatory cell infiltration; however, reduced liver function was only observed in CQD-Spd–treated mice. The in vitro results revealed that only CQDs-Spd significantly decreased the number of viable HepG2 cells (NADH depletion) and induced oxidative stress (heme oxygenase-1 activation) after 24 h of exposure, which promoted inflammatory factor secretion (NF-κB activation). Additionally, decreasing zonula occludens-2 and α1-antitrypsin protein expression in HepG2 cells suggested that CQD-Spd exposure increases the risk of liver diseases. Our results revealed that CQDs-Spd had greater hepatotoxic potential than CQDs-AC and CQDs-AC/Spd, which might be attributable to their high positive surface charge. Overall, the risk of CQD-induced hepatotoxic risk must be considered when applying positively charged CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Oxidative stress
Liver damage
Surface charged
α1-antitrypsin deficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and albumin (ALB)
CQDs induced injury in the liver of BALB/c mice
Cytotoxic effects after exposure to CQDs in HepG2 cells
Oxidative and inflammatory responses after exposure to CQDs in HepG2 cells
Hepatic dysfunction after exposure to CQDs in HepG2 cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology ( MOST , Taiwan) [grant number 110-2811-E-150-005 and 111-2622-E-150-003 ] and the Postdoctoral Fellowship Matching Fund Scheme (No. RG 82/2021-2022R ) of The Education University of Hong Kong. This manuscript was edited by Enago.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterisation of the physicochemical properties of CQDs. (A) TEM image of CQDs-AC. (B) TEM image of CQDs-Spd. (C) TEM image of CQDs-AC/Spd. (D) Zeta potentials of CQDs.
Characterisation of the physicochemical properties of CQDs. (A) XRD patterns of CQDs. (B) FTIR patterns of CQDs.
Change in hepatic enzyme activities in serum by CQDs. (A) AST. (B) ALT. (C) ALB. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with vehicle-treated mice.

Histopathology of liver in CQDs-treated mice. (A) H&E staining for vehicle-treated mice. (B) H&E staining for CQDs-AC-treated mice. (C) H&E staining for CQDs-Spd-treated mice. White arrow represents liver steatosis. (D) Higher magnification of H&E staining for CQDs-Spd-treated mice [the white circle marked in (C)]. (E) H&E staining for CQDs-AC/Spd-treated mice. Red arrow represents mixed inflammatory cell infiltration. (F) Higher magnification of H&E staining for CQDs-AC/Spd-treated mice [the red circle marked in (E)].
Effect of exposure to CQDs on cytotoxicity in HepG2 cells. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control.

Effect of exposure to CQDs on oxidative and inflammatory response in HepG2 cells. (A) HO-1 protein expression. (B) Quantitative analysis of HO-1 protein expression. (C) NF-κB protein expression. (D) Quantitative analysis of NF-κB protein expression The experiments were conducted independently three times. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control.

Effect of exposure to CQDs on cell barrier integrity and predictive biomarkers for liver diseases in HepG2 cells. (A) ZO-2 protein expression. (B) Quantitative analysis of ZO-2 protein expression. (C) AAT protein expression. (D) Quantitative analysis of AAT protein expression. The experiments were conducted independently three times. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control.

head.ce:title: Hepatotoxicity evaluations of different surface charged carbon quantum dots in vivo and in vitro

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become popular because of their simple synthesis and potential applications. Although CQDs have high biocompatibility, their biotoxicity must be verified to reduce the possible risks associated with large-scale application. In this study, the hepatotoxicity of three CQD types, namely diammonium citrate (AC)-based (CQDs-AC), spermidine trihydrochloride (Spd)-based (CQDs-Spd), and AC- and Spd-based CQDs (CQDs-AC/Spd), were evaluated in vivo and in vitro. It was observed in vivo that CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd, but not CQDs-AC, caused histopathological damage, including liver steatosis and mild mixed inflammatory cell infiltration; however, reduced liver function was only observed in CQD-Spd–treated mice. The in vitro results revealed that only CQDs-Spd significantly decreased the number of viable HepG2 cells (NADH depletion) and induced oxidative stress (heme oxygenase-1 activation) after 24 h of exposure, which promoted inflammatory factor secretion (NF-κB activation). Additionally, decreasing zonula occludens-2 and α1-antitrypsin protein expression in HepG2 cells suggested that CQD-Spd exposure increases the risk of liver diseases. Our results revealed that CQDs-Spd had greater hepatotoxic potential than CQDs-AC and CQDs-AC/Spd, which might be attributable to their high positive surface charge. Overall, the risk of CQD-induced hepatotoxic risk must be considered when applying positively charged CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were discovered by Xu et al. through electrophoresis purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1]. CQDs are novel carbon nanomaterials with a zero-dimensional structure. Structurally, CQDs often have a spherical appearance, and they consist of amorphous carbon and sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon atoms [2]. CQDs are prepared from several carbon sources, such as organic solvents, biomass, and graphite particles [3]. The synthesis methods for CQDs can be generally classified into two approaches, namely top-down and bottom-up [4]. The bottom-up approach uses carbon-containing compounds as the starting material, and synthesis is performed via pyrolysis or carbonization. The main methods include sintering [5], microwave-assisted synthesis [6], and hydrothermal synthesis [7]. The top-down approach uses more carbon materials as the starting materials, and chemical, physical, or electrochemical means are to degrade them into CQDs [8]. The main methods include arc discharge [9], chemical oxidation[10], laser ablation [11], electrochemical oxidation [12], and ultrasound synthesis[13].

CQDs possess many unique physical and chemical characteristics, such as high water solubility, good electrochemical characteristics, photoinduced electron transfer, easy functionalization, and biocompatibility. Therefore, they have attracted extensive attention and application in many fields. In biomedical sciences, CQDs have been widely used in biological imaging and biosensing [14]. As CQDs are fluorescent, they can be used as imaging agents for cell labeling, tumor detection, and tumor treatment in biomedical applications [15]. Furthermore, CQDs can be used as biosensors to detect DNA, glucose, and other biomolecules [16], and they are therefore used in biomedical diagnosis and treatment. Another application is energy storage and conversion. CQDs can be used in solar batteries, in hydrogen production from the electrolysis of water, and in supercapacitors [17]. Specifically, the high surface area and outstanding optical and electrical properties of CQDs permit their use in solar batteries to increase their photoelectric conversion efficiency [18]. In addition, CQDs can also be used for hydrogen production by water electrolysis based on their outstanding photocatalytic ability to promote water degradation and hydrogen release [19]. In addition, CQDs can also be used in supercapacitors because of their high specific capacitance and rapid charge and discharge characteristics [20]. Furthermore, other applications of CQDs include environmental protection and catalysis, in which they are used in cell analysis, water quality testing, and chemical catalysis, providing more possibilities for human life and industrial development.

Although CQDs are widely used in many fields, there is still no definite conclusion on their risk of harm to the human body. Some studies showed that CQDs have high biocompatibility [21], and binding to CQDs can decrease the toxicity of specific composite materials [22]. However, as CQDs have complex and diverse structural and chemical characteristics, some studies found that they are toxic to cells. Esfandiari et al. used different temperatures and carbonization durations to synthesize CQDs and assessed their cytotoxicity in human breast cancer SK-BR-3 cells and human breast epithelial MCF-12A cells. Their results showed that CQDs synthesized at 160 ℃ decreased cell viability by 70% [23]. Yan et al. synthesized CQDs with different surface potentials and examined their cytotoxicity in human umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs). The results illustrated that CQDs caused significant cytotoxicity at concentrations of 100–400 µg/mL and decreased the adipogenic and osteogenic differentiation capacity of hUCMSCs [24]. In addition, Liu et al. used cervical cancer HeLa cells, human embryonic kidney HEK293 cells, and human liver cancer HepG2 cells to evaluate the cytoplasm of CQDs and found that photoinduction can significantly increase the cytotoxicity of CQDs in the aforementioned cells [25]. Based on the aforementioned findings, it can be deduced that CQDs prepared using different carbon sources and synthesis methods have different surface structures and characteristics (carbonization degree, surface potential), leading to differences cytotoxic potential. Therefore, sufficient toxicity risk assessment must be performed before CQD use to decrease their potential health risks to the human body and environment.

In this study, we investigated whether CQD exposure increased the risk of hepatotoxicity. In vivo and in vitro experiments were performed to compare the toxic impacts and relevant biological effector mechanisms of different types of CQDs (diammonium citrate [AC]-based CQDs [CQDs-AC], spermidine trihydrochloride [Spd]-based CQDs [CQDs-Spd], and AC- and Spd-based CQDs [CQDs-AC/Spd]). These findings are anticipated to clarify the contribution of different types of CQDs to the pathogenesis of hepatotoxicity. Our study will be important for evaluating and understanding hepatotoxicity caused by CQD exposure.

CQDs were produced through carbonization-activation process based on previous research [26]. AC, Spd, and an AC/Spd mixture (mass ratio 1:1) were ground and heated at 180 °C. The cooling residues were dissolved in ddH2O and sonicated. After centrifugation, the supernatants were collected and then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane. Finally, the supernatants were freeze-dried and dissolved in ddH2O until the test was conducted. The shape and size of CQDs were characterized by field emission transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, TYO, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of CQDs were recorded via spectrophotometer (Jasco, OK, USA) and X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical, Worcestershire, UK), respectively. The zeta potentials of the CQDs were determined using the Zetasizer Nanosystem (Malvern Panalytical, MA, USA).

Male BALB/c mice, 8–12 weeks old, were purchased from National Laboratory Animal Center (TPE, Taiwan) and acclimated for 2 weeks in the animal facilities at National Formosa University (YUN, Taiwan). The BALB/c mice, serving as experimental animal models, have found extensive application in scientific research and are well-supported by a wealth of literature. Their biological characteristics and immune system responses are of significant value in the assessment of toxicity, as they can furnish crucial insights into the impact of drugs or substances on organisms. BALB/c mice offer a dependable platform for evaluating the acute toxicity response to specific substances, aiding in the determination of potential safety risks. All animal use protocols were approved by the Animal Control Committee of National Formosa University (A109–1). Mice were maintained under a reversed light/dark cycle (12 h/12 h), relative humidity of 41% ± 2%, and temperature of 23 ℃ ± 1 °C, and water and food were available ad libitum. Three mice (n = 6) were randomly selected for each group. Mice were given one dose of CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, or CQDs-AC/Spd (2 g/kg) by oral gavage. Mice were sacrificed at 24 h after dosing. Livers were collected for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and albumin (ALB)
ce:section.ce:para: Serum was isolated from CQDs-treated mouse blood and then analyzed. The ALT, AST, and ALB activities in the serum of mice in each group (n = 6) were analyzed by Vitro 350 autoanalyzer (Johnson & Johnson Co., NJ, USA) with accompanying commercial kits.

Liver tissue specimens were fixed in buffered formalin (10%) prior to dehydration and paraffin wax embedding. Sections were cut at a thickness of 3 mm using a rotary microtome. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain was used based on standard H&E staining protocol.
HepG2 human liver carcinoma cells were maintained in DMEM in a humidified CO2 incubator (5% CO2) at 37 °C. The DMEM growth medium was changed three times per week, and cell was trypsinized weekly.

CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd were dispersed in DMEM growth medium, and their cytotoxic effect was tested by Cell Counting Kit-8 (Dojindo, MD, USA). HepG2 cells were seeded 8 × 103 cells/well in a 96-well plate and reacted with different CQDs (0.1–100 µg/mL) for 24 h. CCK-8 solution (10 µL/well) was added and the 96-well plate was incubated at 37 ℃. Before sample testing, we will perform centrifugation to eliminate the potential influence of CQDs on the CCK-8 toxicity assay. The absorbance of CCK-8 test was read by microplate (450 nm). Experiments were performed in triplicate independently.

The oxidative stress-, inflammatory- and liver disease-related protein expressions were determined by western blotting. HepG2 cells were seeded 1.42 × 104 cells in 10-cm dishes and treated with CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, or CQDs-AC/Spd (10 and 100 µg/mL) for 24 h. HepG2 cells were lysed in RIPA buffer at 4 °C and the cellular protein was collected. Protein concentrations were determined by BCA assay with BCA Protein Kit (Visual Protein, TPE, Taiwan). Proteins were mixed with loading dye, separated using a 10% sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) polyacrylamide gel, and electroblotted onto membranes. Anti-heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) (Cell Signaling Technology, MA, USA), anti–NF-κB p65 (Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), anti-zonula occluden-2 (ZO-2; Santa Cruz, Inc., CA, USA), anti–α1-antitrypsin (AAT; Invitrogen, MA, USA), and anti–β-actin monoclonal antibodies (Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) were used at 1:500 to 1:5000 dilutions. The membranes were incubated in solution containing anti-rabbit or anti-mouse HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies (1:5000). The signals were detected using an enhanced chemiluminescent detection assay (GE Healthcare, IL, USA). Beta-actin was used as a loading control. Experiments were performed in triplicate independently.

All experiments were performed three times independently. Statistical Analysis was performed using one-way ANOVA, followed by Dunnett’s multiple comparison tests. p < 0.05 was considered significantly different.

In this study, physicochemical analysis of CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd was first performed. The FE-TEM results showed that the particle size of the three CQDs was smaller than 10 nm (CQDs-AC: 6.3 ± 1.7 nm, CQDs-Spd: 5.6 ± 1.3 nm and CQDs-AC/Spd: 5.2 ± 2.0 nm), and they all displayed a regular lattice arrangement ( Fig. 1). The distance between two CQDs-AC lattices was 0.21 nm (Fig. 1A), which corresponds to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite carbon. The surface material and graphite carbon exhibited similar crystallinity [27]. As CQDs-Spd contained chlorine functional groups, their lattice distance was increased to 0.25 (Fig. 1B) [28]. The distance between two CQDs-AC/Spd lattices was 0.26 nm (Fig. 1C), which was similar to that for CQDs-Spd. The mean zeta potentials of CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd in solution were − 33, 42.2, and 26.8 mV, respectively (Fig. 1D). In general, nanoparticles with zeta potentials greater than + 30 mV or lower than − 30 mV have better stability. When the zeta potential of nanoparticles is lower than + 25 mV or greater than − 25 mV, interactions between nanoparticles (including van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds) would ultimately cause uniformly dispersed particles prone to aggregation [29]. The pH values of CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd in ddH2O were 8.63, 8.09 and 8.56.

This study employed XRD to determine the crystal structure of CQDs. The characteristic peak for graphite (002) was observed at 2θ = 21.5° for CQDs-AC ( Fig. 2) [27]. The corresponding graphite (002) characteristic peak appeared at 2θ = 26° for CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd (Fig. 2) [30]. The peaks at 21.5 and 26° were classified as graphite characteristic peaks (Fig. 2A). In addition to the characteristics of the material itself, the dopant atoms replaced the atoms of the original structure or entered the original crystal structure, and a shift in the diffraction angle occurred. The Cl content of CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd was higher than that of CQDs-AC, which might be the main reason for the diffraction peak shift. In addition, CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd showed the same diffraction peaks as the NH4Cl standard spectrum at 2θ = 23.1, 32.9, 47.0, 52.9, 58.5, and 68.6° (Fig. 2A) [31].

To elucidate the chemical structures of the three CQDs, FTIR was used for qualitative analysis. CQDs-AC had C-N and N-H functional groups at 1160 and 1600 cm−1 (Fig. 2B), respectively, indicating amine stretching vibration and bending vibration [32]. The absorption peak of CQDs-Spd at 850 cm−1 denoted the presence of the C-Cl functional group (Fig. 2B), indicating that halogen compounds were undergoing stretching vibration [33]. The 1633 cm−1 absorption peak of CQDs-AC indicated bending vibration of the N-H2 group in amines (Fig. 2B) [34]. CQDs-AC/Spd basically featured the characteristic peaks of CQDs-AC and CQDs-Spd. The three CQDs all contained nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and exhibited phenolic O–H functional group stretching at 3200–3640 cm−1 (Fig. 2B) [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs induced injury in the liver of BALB/c mice
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the hepatotoxicity of CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd, an acute toxicity (2 g/kg) test was performed in BALB/c mice. The dosage of 2 g/kg is considered a common starting dose for acute toxicity testing, which helps in the initial assessment of the substance's toxic potential. Our study aimed to preliminarily assess the acute toxicity response of CQDs in mice to understand their potential safety and risks. Therefore, we chose a relatively high dosage to increase the chances of observing toxic effects. This helped ensure that our study could detect potential toxicity signals and lay the foundation for further toxicity assessments. Blood was collected after 24 h for AST, ALT, and ALB analysis. AST and ALT mainly control amino acid conversion and metabolism, and they are released when hepatocytes die from stimulation caused by viral infection, alcohol, or other substances [36]. The test results showed that CQDs-Spd, but not CQDs-AC or CQDs-AC/Spd, significantly induced AST and ALT elevation ( Figs. 3A and 3B). ALB can be used to evaluate protein synthesis in the liver, in which the blood albumin concentration significantly decreases in cases of severe disease [37]. The study results illustrated that CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd do not cause blood ALB levels to significantly decrease (Fig. 3C). The blood analysis results showed that only CQDs-Spd caused liver damage (Fig. 3).

Tissue sections were used for pathological interpretation to further determine the risk of liver damage induced by CQDs in mice. The experimental results revealed that CQDs-AC did not induce any lesions in mouse livers ( Fig. 4B), whereas liver steatosis and mild mixed inflammatory cell infiltration were observed in mice treated with CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd, respectively (Figs. 4C and 4D). Cytoplasmic vesicles are replaced in steatosis, and this can cause damage to cells once a large amount is accumulated [38]. Mixed inflammatory responses result in milder stimulation and indicate that the liver is currently under attacked by foreign substances with low stimulation [39]. Overall, short-term exposure to CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd might result in a risk of liver impairment, whereas the effects of CQDs-AC/Spd do not appear to be strong. The probability of liver damage caused by long-term exposure to CQDs could not be dismissed in this study, particularly long-term CQDs-Spd exposure, which has a high risk of inducing liver disease (Fig. 4C).

While the histological examination revealed signs of hepatic steatosis and mild mixed inflammatory cell infiltration in both CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd-treated mice (Figs. 4C and D), it was only in the CQDs-Spd group that a notable elevation in serum AST and ALT levels was observed (Figs. 3A and B). Such differences may be related to the timing and manifestation of toxic effects, with some effects manifesting earlier or being more easily detectable than others. Although we observed liver steatosis and mild mixed inflammatory cell infiltration, it is possible that these pathological changes may not be sufficient to cause a significant increase in serum AST and ALT levels. The degree of liver damage may have a certain threshold relationship with serum biochemical indicators, requiring more severe damage to trigger a significant biochemical response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxic effects after exposure to CQDs in HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To further compare the differences in hepatocyte toxicity and the mechanism of action among the three CQDs, cytotoxicity tests were further performed using HepG2 cells. First, CCK-8 reagent was used to test the effects of CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd (0.1, 1, 10, and 100 μg/mL) on the viability of HepG2 cells. After 24 h of treatment, only 100 μg/mL CQDs-Spd significantly decreased the viability of HepG2 cells, whereas there were no differences in cell viability between the control group and the AC-CQD and Ac/Spd-CQD groups ( Fig. 5). This might be attributable to the strong positive charge on the Spd-CQD surfaces. Generally, nanoparticles with positively charged surfaces will cause greater cytotoxicity, severe cell injury, and death [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative and inflammatory responses after exposure to CQDs in HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Excessive ROS accumulation might cause cell death or induce disease [41]. HO-1 protein expression is an important cellular response to oxidative stress [42]. Therefore, the differences in HO-1 protein expression after 24 h of CQD treatment (10 and 100 μg/mL) in HepG2 cells were analyzed. The study results illustrated that CQDs-Spd and CQDs-AC/Spd significantly increased HO-1 expression, whereas there was no significant difference between the AC-CQD and control groups ( Figs. 6A and B). An increase in HO-1 expression could help alleviate oxidative stress in cells [43]. This study revealed that CQDs-Spd could strongly induce oxidative stress in HepG2 cells.

As ROS plays a crucial role in regulating cell inflammatory responses, the levels of related pro-inflammatory factors were tested in this study. HepG2 cells were treated with CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, or CQDs-AC/Spd (10 and 100 μg/mL) for 24 h. NF-κB is a protein complex that controls DNA transcription, and it is present in the cytoplasm. NF-κB can be activated via stimulation by inflammatory cytokines and ROS [44]. In this experiment, western blotting was used to analyze NF-κB expression. The results illustrated that only 100 μg/mL CQDs-Spd induced significant NF-κB upregulation in HepG2 cells (Figs. 6C and D). Prior research proved that excessive intracellular ROS accumulation could increase NF-κB expression, thereby decreasing AAT expression, which ultimately causes liver damage and chronic hepatitis [45,46]. From previous results, it can be observed that CQDs-Spd carry a high risk of oxidative toxicity in hepatocytes (Figs. 6A and B). Exposure of HepG2 cells to CQDs-Spd can induce significant oxidative (HO-1) and inflammatory (NF-kB) responses, ultimately causing cell death (Figs. 5 and 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hepatic dysfunction after exposure to CQDs in HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to stabilizing the connections between cells, tight junctions can maintain substance transport between cells and the osmotic equilibrium [47]. Related studies illustrated that excessive ROS accumulation and inflammatory factor activation disrupts tight junctions in various epithelial tissues [48]. In this study, western blotting was used to measure the expression of tight junction proteins (zonula occludens 2, ZO-2) in HepG2 cells. The results showed that CQDs-Spd induced significant ZO-2 downregulation in HepG2 cells ( Figs. 7A and B). This illustrated that CQDs-Spd had little risk of disrupting the hepatocyte membrane barrier. Research has shown that there is a mutual dependency between ZO-2 and other tight junction proteins, and they work together to ensure the integrity of the cell membrane barrier [49]. This collaboration allows the cell membrane barrier to effectively prevent harmful substances from entering tissues. Therefore, any abnormal functioning or deficiency in the expression of ZO-2 may lead to damage to the cell membrane barrier, thereby adversely affecting the health of tissues and organs [50]. In studies related to specific liver diseases, it has been observed that the lack of expression of ZO-2 is directly associated with certain liver diseases [51].

AAT is mainly synthesized by the liver, and it is an important protease inhibitor in the human body, as it can bind to many proteases to inhibit their activities, thereby preventing excessive protease activation. In this experiment, western blotting was used to analyze AAT expression. The results showed that CQDs-Spd, but not AC-CQD and CQDs-AC/Spd, induced significant AAT downregulation in HepG2 cells (Figs. 7C and D). Generally, decreased liver AAT expression increases the risk [46,52] of diseases such as chronic liver disease and cirrhosis [53]. In addition, abnormal AAT protein synthesized in the liver accumulates in the endoplasmic reticulum and induces liver damage [46,52]. These results revealed showed that exposure to CQDs-Spd may increases the risk of liver disease.

This study elucidated the differences in hepatotoxicity among three CQDs (CQDs-AC, CQDs-Spd, and CQDs-AC/Spd). From the blood test and pathological section results, only CQDs-Spd induced mild liver damage in mice. Similar results were observed in HepG2 cell toxicity tests. Specifically, Spd-CQD exposure induced oxidative stress and inflammation in HepG2 cells and further inhibited AAT expression. Overall, CQD-induced hepatotoxicity appears to be associated with its surface charge. Because of their surface amine groups, CQDs-Spd are positively charged, which facilitates their adsorption to the cell membrane and increases hepatocyte entry efficiency. This ultimately leads to significant hepatocyte toxicity and an increased risk of liver disease. The risk of CQDs-AC/Spd-induced liver damage cannot be completely dismissed according to the experimental data obtained in this study because hepatocytes might be exposed to CQDs-AC/Spd with positive surface charges for long periods, leading to a higher cumulative dose, thereby inducing hepatotoxicity. People have to pay more attention to the health problems associated with positively charged CQD usage.

Chen Hung-Hsiang: Investigation. Chen Yi-Chun: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Huang Chih-Ching: Methodology, Visualization. Lin Han-Jia: Methodology, Visualization. Lin Chia-Hua: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Lin Kun-Yi Andrew: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Peng Yen-Ping: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Chen Yan-Hua: Investigation. Tsang Yiu Fai: Investigation, Resources. Chen Ku-Fan: Investigation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST, Taiwan) [grant number 110-2811-E-150-005 and 111-2622-E-150-003] and the Postdoctoral Fellowship Matching Fund Scheme (No. RG 82/2021-2022R) of The Education University of Hong Kong. This manuscript was edited by Enago.
